# Exodus Chapter 35 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 106–107; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 78–79 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_035_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_035_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_035_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_035_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Sabbath Command (vv. 1–3)

**1** And Moses assembled [**vayyaqhel Mosheh**] all [**et-kol**] the congregation of [**adat**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**], and he said [**vayyomer**] to them [**aleihem**]: "These [**elleh**] are the words [**haddevarim**] which [**asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] has commanded [**tsivvah**] to do [**la'asot**] them [**otam**]:

**2** "Six [**sheshet**] days [**yamim**] work [**melakhah**] shall be done [**te'aseh**], and on the seventh [**uvayom hashevi'i**] day [**yihyeh**] it shall be [**yihyeh**] to you [**lakhem**] a holy thing [**qodesh**], a Sabbath [**Shabbat**] of complete rest [**Shabbaton**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**]; everyone [**kol**] who does [**ha'oseh**] work [**melakhah**] on it [**bo**] shall be put to death [**yumat**].

**3** "You shall not [**lo**] kindle [**teva'aru**] a fire [**esh**] in all [**bekhol**] your dwelling places [**moshevotekhem**] on the day of [**beyom**] the Sabbath [**haShabbat**]."

---

## The Offering for the Tabernacle: Moses' Call (vv. 4–9)

**4** And Moses said [**vayyomer Mosheh**] to [**el**] all [**kol**] the congregation of [**adat**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**], saying [**lemor**]: "This [**zeh**] is the word [**haddavar**] which [**asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] has commanded [**tsivvah**], saying [**lemor**]:

**5** "Take [**qechu**] from among you [**me'ittkhem**] a contribution [**terumah**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**]; every [**kol**] willing one of [**nediv**] heart [**libbo**] shall bring it [**yevi'eha**] — the contribution of [**et terumot**] YHWH [**YHWH**]: gold [**zahav**] and silver [**vakhesef**] and bronze [**unechoshet**],

**6** "and blue [**utekhelet**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet of [**vetola'at**] crimson [**shani**] and fine linen [**veshesh**] and goats' hair [**ve'izzim**],

**7** "and rams' [**ve'orot eylim**] skins [**ve'orot**] dyed red [**me'oddamim**] and tahash [**ve'orot techashim**] skins [**ve'orot**] and acacia [**va'atsey**] wood [**shittim**],

**8** "and oil [**veshemen**] for the light [**lamma'or**] and spices [**uvesamim**] for the anointing [**leshemen hammishchah**] oil [**leshemen**] and for the fragrant [**veliktoret**] incense [**hassammim**],

**9** "and onyx [**ve'avney**] stones [**shoham**] and setting [**ve'avney**] stones [**millu'im**] for the ephod [**la'efod**] and for the breastpiece [**velachoshen**]."

---

## The Call to Skilled Workers (vv. 10–19)

**10** "And every [**vekhol**] wise one of [**chakham**] heart [**lev**] among you [**bakhem**] shall come [**yavo'u**] and make [**veya'asu**] all [**et kol**] that [**asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] has commanded [**tsivvah**]:

**11** "the Tabernacle [**et-hammishkan**], its tent [**et-oholo**] and its covering [**et-mikhseyhu**], its clasps [**et-qerasav**] and its boards [**et-qerashav**], its bars [**et-berichav**], its pillars [**et-ammudav**] and its bases [**ve'et-adanav**];

**12** "the Ark [**et-ha'aron**] and its poles [**ve'et-baddav**], the mercy seat [**et-hakkaporet**] and the veil of [**ve'et parokhet**] the screen [**hammasakh**];

**13** "the table [**et-hashulchan**] and its poles [**ve'et-baddav**] and all [**ve'et-kol**] its vessels [**kelav**] and the bread of [**ve'et lechem**] the Presence [**happanim**];

**14** "and the lampstand of [**ve'et-menorat**] the light [**hamma'or**] and its vessels [**ve'et-keleha**] and its lamps [**ve'et-neroteiha**] and the oil of [**ve'et shemen**] the light [**hamma'or**];

**15** "and the altar of [**ve'et-mizbach**] the incense [**haqetoret**] and its poles [**ve'et-baddav**] and the anointing [**ve'et shemen hammishchah**] oil [**shemen**] and the fragrant [**ve'et ketoret**] incense [**hassammim**] and the entrance [**ve'et-masakh**] screen [**happetach**] for the entrance of [**lefetach**] the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**];

**16** "the altar of [**et mizbach**] the burnt offering [**ha'olah**] and the bronze [**hannechoshet**] grating [**et-mikbar**] which [**asher**] belongs to it [**lo**], its poles [**et-baddav**] and all [**ve'et-kol**] its vessels [**kelav**]; the basin [**et-hakkiyyor**] and its stand [**ve'et-kanno**];

**17** "the hangings of [**et qal'ey**] the court [**hechatser**], its pillars [**et-ammudav**] and its bases [**ve'et-adaneyha**] and the screen of [**ve'et masakh**] the gate of [**sha'ar**] the court [**hechatser**];

**18** "the pegs of [**et-yitdot**] the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**] and the pegs of [**ve'et-yitdot**] the court [**hechatser**] and their cords [**ve'et-meytreyhem**];

**19** "the woven garments [**et-bigdey hasserad**] for ministering [**lesharet**] in the holy place [**baqqodesh**], the holy [**et-bigdey haqqodesh**] garments [**bigdey**] for Aaron [**le'Aharon**] the priest [**hakkohen**] and the garments of [**ve'et-bigdey**] his sons [**vanav**] for serving as priests [**lekhahhen**]."

---

## The People's Willing Response (vv. 20–29)

**20** And all [**vayyetse'u kol**] the congregation of [**adat**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**] went out [**vayyetse'u**] from the presence of [**milllifney**] Moses [**Mosheh**].

**21** And they came [**vayyavo'u**], every [**kol**] man [**ish**] whose [**asher**] heart [**lev**] stirred him [**nesa'o libbo**], and every one [**vekhol**] whose [**asher**] spirit [**ruach**] made him willing [**nadevah oto rucho**], they brought [**hevi'u**] the contribution of [**et-terumat**] YHWH [**YHWH**] for the work of [**limle'khet**] the Tent of [**ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**] and for all [**ulekhol**] its service [**avodato**] and for the holy [**ulevigdey**] garments [**haqqodesh**].

**22** And they came [**vayyavo'u**], the men [**ha'anashim**] with [**al**] the women [**hannashim**], every [**kol**] willing one of [**nediv**] heart [**lev**]; they brought [**hevi'u**] brooches [**chach**] and earrings [**vanezem**] and rings [**vetabba'at**] and ornaments [**vekhumaz**], every [**kol**] article [**keli**] of gold [**zahav**]; and every [**vekhol**] man [**ish**] who [**asher**] waved [**henif**] a wave offering of [**tenufat**] gold [**zahav**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**].

**23** And every [**vekhol**] man [**ish**] who [**asher**] was found [**nimtsa**] with him [**itto**] blue [**tekhelet**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet of [**vetola'at**] crimson [**shani**] and fine linen [**veshesh**] and goats' hair [**ve'izzim**] and rams' [**ve'orot eylim**] skins [**ve'orot**] dyed red [**me'oddamim**] and tahash [**ve'orot techashim**] skins [**ve'orot**] — they brought them [**hevi'u**].

**24** Every one [**kol**] lifting [**merim**] a contribution of [**terumat**] silver [**kesef**] and bronze [**unechoshet**] brought [**hevi'u**] the contribution of [**et terumat**] YHWH [**YHWH**]; and every one [**vekhol**] who [**asher**] was found [**nimtsa**] with him [**itto**] acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**] for any [**lekhol**] work of [**mele'khet**] the service [**ha'avodah**] — they brought it [**hevi'u**].

**25** And every [**vekhol**] woman [**ishah**] wise of [**chakhmat**] heart [**lev**] spun [**tavu**] with her hands [**beyadeyha**], and they brought [**vayyavi'u**] the spun yarn [**matve**]: the blue [**et-hattekhelet**] and the purple [**ve'et-ha'argaman**] and the scarlet of [**et-tola'at**] crimson [**hasshani**] and the fine linen [**ve'et-hashesh**].

**26** And all [**vekhol**] the women [**hannashim**] whose [**asher**] heart [**libban**] stirred [**nasa**] them [**otanah**] in wisdom [**bechokmah**] spun [**tavu**] the goats' hair [**et-ha'izzim**].

**27** And the leaders [**vehannesiy'im**] brought [**hevi'u**] the onyx [**et avney**] stones [**hashoham**] and the setting [**ve'et avney**] stones [**hammillu'im**] for the ephod [**la'efod**] and for the breastpiece [**velachoshen**],

**28** and the spice [**ve'et-habesamim**] and the oil [**ve'et-hashamen**] for the light [**lamma'or**] and for the anointing [**uleshemen hammishchah**] oil [**shemen**] and for the fragrant [**veliktoret**] incense [**hassammim**].

**29** Every [**kol**] man [**ish**] and woman [**ve'ishah**] whose [**asher**] heart [**libam**] made them willing [**nadav otam**] to bring [**lehavi**] for all [**lekhol**] the work [**hammelakhah**] which [**asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] had commanded [**tsivvah**] to be done [**la'asot**] by the hand of [**beyad**] Moses [**Mosheh**] — the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**] brought [**hevi'u**] a freewill offering [**nedavah**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**].

---

## The Appointment of Bezalel and Oholiab (vv. 30–35)

**30** And Moses said [**vayyomer Mosheh**] to [**el**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**]: "See [**re'u**], YHWH [**YHWH**] has called [**qara**] by name [**veshem**] Bezalel [**Betsal'el**] son of [**ben**] Uri [**Uri**], son of [**ben**] Hur [**Chur**], of the tribe of [**lematteh**] Judah [**Yehudah**].

**31** "And He has filled [**vayemalle**] him [**oto**] with the Spirit of [**ruach**] God [**Elohim**], in wisdom [**bechokmah**], in understanding [**bitvunah**] and in knowledge [**uveda'at**] and in all [**uvekhol**] craftsmanship [**melakhah**],

**32** "and to devise [**velachashov**] designs [**machashavot**], to work [**la'asot**] in gold [**bazzahav**] and in silver [**uvakkesef**] and in bronze [**uvannechoshet**],

**33** "and in cutting of [**uvacharoshet**] stone [**even**] for setting [**lemalle**] and in cutting of [**uvacharoshet**] wood [**ets**], to work [**la'asot**] in every [**bekhol**] craft of [**mele'khet**] design [**machashevet**].

**34** "And to teach [**ulehorot**] He has put [**natan**] in his heart [**belibbo**] — he [**hu**] and Oholiab [**ve'Oholiy'av**] son of [**ben**] Ahisamach [**Achisamakh**], of the tribe of [**lematteh**] Dan [**Dan**].

**35** "He has filled them [**mille otam**] with wisdom of [**chokhmat**] heart [**lev**] to do [**la'asot**] all [**kol**] work of [**mele'khet**] a craftsman [**charash**] and of a designer [**vechoshev**] and of an embroiderer [**veroqem**] in the blue [**battekhelet**] and in the purple [**uva'argaman**] and in the scarlet of [**betola'at**] crimson [**hasshani**] and in the fine linen [**uvashesh**] and of a weaver [**ve'oreg**] — doers of [**osey**] all [**kol**] work [**melakhah**] and devisers of [**vechoshshevey**] designs [**machashavot**]."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

1. **Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 106–107:** The text of Exodus 35 begins on folio 106 (page 1 image), continuing from the conclusion of chapter 34. The parashah petuchah opening is visible, marking the beginning of Parashat Vayaqhel. The text is laid out in the standard three-column format with Tiberian vowel pointing and cantillation marks fully intact throughout. The Sabbath command (vv. 1–3) occupies a compact unit near the top of the right column. The materials list (vv. 5–9) and skilled-workers call (vv. 10–19) run through the central and left columns of page 106. The people's response (vv. 20–29) and the Bezalel/Oholiab appointment (vv. 30–35) continue across page 107. A masorah parva note appears beside the rare verb "vayyaqhel" in v. 1. The word "nedavah" (freewill offering) in v. 29 carries distinctive cantillation (zaqef qatan), emphasizing the willing character of the offering.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 78–79:** The Greek text of Exodus 35 (LXX chapter heading visible as KH on page 78) follows the standard Vaticanus three-column uncial format in scriptio continua. The LXX arrangement of Exodus 35–40 diverges significantly from the MT. Vaticanus follows the longer LXX recension, which reorders the Tabernacle construction material. In the MT, chapter 35 closely mirrors the earlier command sections (chs. 25–31), creating a command-fulfillment parallel. The LXX rearranges elements and contains some expansions. Notably, the LXX of v. 1 reads "sunegagen Mouses pasan sunagogen huion Israel" (Moses gathered together the whole congregation of the sons of Israel), using sunagoge rather than ekklesia for the assembly. The materials list in the LXX tracks the MT closely, though with minor variations in the order of items. The appointment of Bezalel (vv. 30–35) in Vaticanus corresponds to the MT but with the characteristic Greek expansion of the Spirit-filling description.

### Key Translation Decisions

3. **v. 1 — "vayyaqhel" (and he assembled):** The Hiphil form of the root קהל (qahal), meaning to assemble or gather together. This verb gives the parashah its name (Vayaqhel). The root qahal yields the noun "qahal" (assembly/congregation), a term of immense theological significance — the gathered community of YHWH. Moses actively assembles the people, the first such gathering after the golden calf crisis and the covenant renewal of chapter 34. The LXX renders with "sunegagen" (gathered together), using the verb form of sunagoge.

4. **v. 2 — "Shabbat Shabbaton" (a Sabbath of complete rest):** The construction intensifies the noun by doubling it — "Shabbat" (cessation) modified by "Shabbaton" (complete cessation). This is the superlative form: not merely rest, but a rest of rest, absolute cessation. Rendered "a Sabbath of complete rest" to preserve the intensification. The death penalty attached to Sabbath violation ("shall be put to death," yumat — Hophal imperfect of מות) places this command at the highest severity level among the covenant stipulations. The LXX reads "sabbata anapausis hagia to kurio" (a sabbath, a holy rest to the Lord).

5. **v. 3 — "lo teva'aru esh" (you shall not kindle a fire):** The prohibition against kindling fire on the Sabbath is the only specific activity singled out in this Sabbath command. The verb "teva'aru" (Piel imperfect of בער, ba'ar) means to kindle, burn, or cause to blaze. This prohibition, placed immediately before the Tabernacle construction instructions, establishes that even the holy work of building YHWH's dwelling must yield to the Sabbath — a critical ordering principle. The LXX renders "ou kausete pur" (you shall not burn a fire).

6. **v. 5 — "kol nediv libbo" (every willing one of heart):** The phrase "nediv lev" (willing/noble of heart) is the governing principle of the entire Tabernacle offering. Unlike taxation or compulsion, the contributions are to flow from internal willingness. The adjective "nadiv" (willing, generous, noble) from the root נדב (nadav) is repeated throughout the chapter (vv. 5, 21, 22, 29), creating a thematic refrain. This stands in deliberate contrast to the coerced contributions for the golden calf in 32:2–3. The LXX uses "hekousiazetai te kardia" (whose heart is willing).

7. **v. 11 — Tabernacle component list (vv. 11–19):** The list of Tabernacle components mirrors the original construction commands of Exodus 25–31, forming a command-fulfillment literary structure. The items appear in the same hierarchical order: the dwelling itself (v. 11), the Ark and mercy seat (v. 12), the table and bread of the Presence (v. 13), the lampstand (v. 14), the incense altar (v. 15), the burnt-offering altar (v. 16), the court hangings (v. 17), the pegs and cords (v. 18), and the priestly garments (v. 19). This repetitive cataloguing is a deliberate literary device emphasizing obedient correspondence between command and execution.

8. **v. 12 — "et-hakkaporet" (the mercy seat):** The kapporet, from the root כפר (kaphar, to cover/atone), is the golden cover of the Ark where YHWH's presence was manifest between the cherubim. Rendered "mercy seat" following the standard formal convention. The LXX renders "to hilasterion" (the propitiatory), the same term used in Romans 3:25.

9. **v. 19 — "bigdey hasserad" (woven garments):** The term "serad" appears only in the context of the Tabernacle service garments (cf. Exodus 31:10, 39:1, 39:41). Its precise meaning is debated — possibly "plaited" or "woven" garments used in the sanctuary service. The LXX reads "stolai leitourgikai" (liturgical robes), interpreting "serad" as a functional category rather than a material description.

10. **vv. 21–22 — The stirring of heart and spirit:** Two distinct internal movements are described: "nesa'o libbo" (his heart lifted/stirred him, v. 21) and "nadevah oto rucho" (his spirit made him willing, v. 21). The verbs are different — "nasa" (to lift, carry) for the heart, and "nadav" (to impel, make willing) for the spirit. The first describes an upward movement of inner motivation; the second describes the spirit's generosity. Together they portray a complete internal response: the heart is stirred, and the spirit willingly acts. This dual anthropology (lev/heart and ruach/spirit) is characteristic of the Priestly source.

11. **v. 22 — "chach vanezem vetabba'at vekhumaz" (brooches and earrings and rings and ornaments):** Four types of gold jewelry are enumerated. The term "chach" (brooch or clasp) is rare; "nezem" (ring, earring) appeared in the golden calf narrative (32:2–3), creating an implicit contrast — the same type of jewelry surrendered for an idol is now offered for YHWH's dwelling. The term "khumaz" (ornament) is a hapax legomenon in the singular form, possibly a bracelet or body ornament.

12. **v. 25 — "vekhol ishah chakhmat lev" (and every woman wise of heart):** The "wisdom of heart" (chokhmat lev) ascribed to the women spinners is the same term applied to Bezalel and the master craftsmen (v. 35). This is not merely skill but divinely-endowed wisdom — the same root (chakham) that describes Solomon's wisdom. The women's contribution through spinning is given the same theological dignity as the master artisans' work. The LXX renders "kai pasa gune sophe te dianoia" (and every woman wise in understanding).

13. **v. 26 — "asher nasa libban otanah bechokmah" (whose heart stirred them in wisdom):** The feminine forms ("libban" — their heart, feminine; "otanah" — them, feminine) mark these women as a distinct group whose heart-stirring came specifically "in wisdom" (bechokmah). This is the only place in the Tabernacle narrative where wisdom is explicitly connected to the heart-stirring of the women who spun goats' hair — an ordinary material elevated by the wisdom of its makers.

14. **v. 27 — "vehannesiy'im" (and the leaders):** The nesi'im (leaders, princes, chiefs) bring the most precious materials: onyx stones, setting stones, spices, and oil. Their contribution comes last in the narrative sequence, which later rabbinic tradition (Rashi, citing Bamidbar Rabbah) interpreted as a subtle critique — the leaders waited to see what was needed rather than giving first, and so the spelling of "nesiy'im" is defective (missing a yod) in this verse, a detail visible in the Leningrad Codex.

15. **v. 29 — The framing summary:** The verse serves as an inclusio with v. 5, bracketing the entire offering section. The key terms recur: "willing" (nadav), "bring" (hevi'u), "YHWH commanded" (YHWH tsivvah), "by the hand of Moses" (beyad Mosheh). The final phrase "nedavah laYHWH" (a freewill offering to YHWH) encapsulates the theological character of the entire collection: it is a nedavah — entirely voluntary, prompted by the heart, directed to YHWH.

16. **vv. 30–31 — "qara veshem Betsal'el" (called by name Bezalel):** The phrase "called by name" indicates divine election and commissioning. The name Bezalel (betsal'el) means "in the shadow of God" — an artisan who works under divine protection and patronage. The filling "with the Spirit of God" (ruach Elohim) places the artisan's skill in the same category as prophetic inspiration and royal anointing. The four domains of endowment — wisdom (chokmah), understanding (tevunah), knowledge (da'at), and all craftsmanship (kol melakhah) — mirror the four attributes listed in the original appointment (31:3).

17. **v. 34 — "ulehorot natan belibbo" (and to teach He has put in his heart):** The addition of teaching (horot, Hiphil infinitive of ירה, yarah) to Bezalel's endowment goes beyond the original commission in 31:1–5. Bezalel is not merely a craftsman but a teacher of craft — wisdom that multiplies by transmission. The root yarah also yields "Torah" (teaching/instruction), subtly connecting Bezalel's artisan-teaching with the broader Mosaic Torah. The LXX reads "kai proebebase auton didaxai" (and He advanced him to teach).

18. **v. 35 — The fivefold artisan classification:** The verse enumerates five categories of skilled work: craftsman (charash), designer (choshev), embroiderer (roqem), weaver (oreg), and doers of all work/devisers of designs (osey kol melakhah vechoshshevey machashavot). This fivefold taxonomy represents the full range of Tabernacle artistry — from structural fabrication to decorative design. The final phrase "vechoshshevey machashavot" (devisers of designs) forms an inclusio with v. 32 ("velachashov machashavot" — to devise designs), framing the Bezalel/Oholiab section with the theme of creative thought under divine inspiration.

### Theological Architecture of the Chapter

19. **Sabbath before Tabernacle:** The placement of the Sabbath command (vv. 1–3) before the Tabernacle construction instructions (vv. 4–35) is theologically decisive. It establishes that even the most sacred construction project — building YHWH's dwelling place — must yield to the Sabbath. The Sabbath is not merely one commandment among many; it is the framework within which all other work, including holy work, operates. This ordering mirrors the conclusion of the original instructions (31:12–17), creating a literary envelope: Sabbath frames the Tabernacle commands both at the end of the giving (ch. 31) and at the beginning of the execution (ch. 35).

20. **The Willing Heart as Theological Principle:** The chapter's dominant motif is the "willing heart" (nediv lev / nadav lev), appearing in vv. 5, 21, 22, 26, and 29. This stands in deliberate structural and theological contrast to the golden calf narrative (ch. 32), where contributions were also collected from the people but under Aaron's directive and for an illegitimate purpose. In chapter 35, every gift is voluntary, internally motivated, and directed toward YHWH's explicit command. The contrast reveals the Torah's distinction between idolatrous generosity (misdirected willingness) and covenantal generosity (willingness aligned with divine command).

21. **Command-Fulfillment Structure:** Exodus 35–40 constitutes the fulfillment half of the great Tabernacle diptych (commands: chs. 25–31; fulfillment: chs. 35–40). The near-verbatim repetition of materials, dimensions, and specifications is not literary redundancy but a theological statement: Israel obeyed exactly as YHWH commanded. This command-fulfillment pattern is the structural backbone of the Priestly narrative and anticipates the refrain "as YHWH commanded Moses" that will dominate chapters 39–40.

22. **Formal Equivalence Methodology:** This translation maintains strict word-order fidelity to the Hebrew where English syntax permits, preserves all conjunctions (waw-consecutive and waw-copulative), retains the Hebrew clause structure including fronted objects and appositional constructions, provides consistent English glosses for each Hebrew lexeme, and preserves the cataloguing style of the materials and components lists without condensation. YHWH is transliterated throughout per binding conventions. "Sons of Israel" is preserved rather than "Israelites" or "children of Israel." Proper names follow standard transliteration: Bezalel, Oholiab, Ahisamach. The infinitive absolute construction convention is maintained where applicable.
